---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi -- Ch. 15: Multinomial models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
const DATA = let c = filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "../data", "data"]); isempty(c) ? error("StatEcon example data not found; run this notebook from its own folder (examples/Cameron_Trivedi) or the repo root.") : first(c) end
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

_rawval(x) = hasproperty(x, :value) ? x.value : x  # unwrap ReadStatTables.LabeledValue
```

The multinomial-model estimators for this chapter now live in `StatEcon`
(`src/stata_mlogit.jl`, `stata_mprobit.jl`, `stata_ologit.jl`,
`stata_asclogit.jl`, `stata_nlogit.jl`, `stata_asmprobit.jl`,
`stata_mixlogit.jl`, `stata_biprobit.jl`, `stata_nlsur.jl`, …) and are called
directly below:

- multinomial logit — `stata_mlogit` (+ `stata_test_mlogit`,
  `stata_margins_mlogit`, `stata_margins_dydx_mlogit`)
- multinomial probit — `stata_mprobit`; alternative-specific MNP —
  `stata_asmprobit` (with `stata_estat_covariance` / `stata_estat_correlation`)
- ordered logit — `stata_ologit` (+ `stata_margins_dydx_ologit`)
- conditional / alternative-specific logit — `stata_asclogit`, `stata_clogit`
  (with `stata_estat_alternatives`, `stata_estat_mfx_asclogit`)
- nested logit — `stata_nlogit` (+ `stata_nlogitgen`, `stata_nlogittree`)
- mixed (random-parameters) logit — `stata_mixlogit`
- bivariate probit — `stata_biprobit`; nonlinear SUR — `stata_nlsur`
- Stata display / data helpers — `stata_table`, `stata_list`, `stata_encode`,
  `stata_label_list`, `stata_tabulate_two_way`, `stata_estimates_table_compare`

Each estimator's `predict`-style probability routine is a private helper and
is called module-qualified: `StatEcon._c15_mnl_probs`,
`StatEcon._c15_ologit_probs`, `StatEcon._c15_nlogit_probs`,
`StatEcon._c15_clogit_probs`, `StatEcon._c15_biprobit_probs`. The canonical
`stata_summarize`, `stata_tabulate`, `stata_correlate`, and `stata_probit`
(already in `StatEcon`) are reused unchanged.

# 15 Multinomial models

## 15.1 Introduction

## 15.2 Multinomial models overv1ew

### 15.2.1 Probabilities and MEs

### 15.2.2 Maximum likelihood estimation

### 15.2.3 Case-specific and alternative-specific regressors

### 15.2.4 Additive random-utility model

### 15.2.5 Stata multinomial model commands

## 15.3 Multinomial example: Choice of fishing mode

### 15.3.1 Data description

The fishing-mode data come from Herriges and Kling (1999), "Nonlinear Income
Effects in Random Utility Models", *Review of Economics and Statistics*.
Each observation has the chosen mode plus prices and catch rates for all four
alternatives (beach, pier, private, charter).

```{julia}
#=
* File mus15data is from 
* J. A. Herriges and C. L. Kling, 
* "Nonlinear Income Effects in Random Utility Models", 
* Review of Economics and Statistics, 81(1999): 62-72
* Also analyzed in Cameron and Trivedi (2005) chapter 15

* File mus18data.dta is from
* Rand Health Insurance Experiment data 
* Essentially same data as in P. Deb and P.K. Trivedi (2002)
* "The Structure of Demand for Medical Care: Latent Class versus
* Two-Part Models", Journal of Health Economics, 21, 601-625
* except that paper used different outcome (counts rather than $)
* Each observation is for an individual over a year.
* Individuals may appear in up to five years.
* All available sample is used except only fee for service plans included.
* If panel data used then clustering is on id (person id)
=#
# Read in dataset and describe
df15 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus15data.dta"))
DataFrames.describe(df15)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Read in dataset and describe dependent variable and regressors
use musr/mus15data.dta, clear
describe
=#
# Summary
DataFrames.describe(df15, :mean, :std, :min, :max, :nmissing)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Summarize dependent variable and regressors
summarize, separator(0) 
=#
# Tabulate the dependent variable
DataFrames.combine(DataFrames.groupby(df15, :mode), DataFrames.nrow => :freq)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Tabulate the dependent variable
tabulate mode
=#
#=
* Summarize dependent variable and regressors
summarize, separator(0)
=#

# Stata's `summarize` with no varlist summarizes *all* variables in
# memory; `separator(0)` suppresses the dashed line Stata draws every
# 5 rows of a long varlist. The chunk-1 `stata_summarize` helper does
# not emit that separator by default, so the option is a no-op here —
# the only effective change is widening the table to every column of
# `df15`.
stata_summarize(df15);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Tabulate the dependent variable
tabulate mode
=#

# `stata_tabulate` (chunk 1) is Stata's one-way `tabulate`: sorts by
# the raw labeled-value key (so categories appear in `1 = beach,
# 2 = pier, 3 = private, 4 = charter` order) and prints the standard
# Freq / Percent / Cum table with a trailing Total row.
stata_tabulate(df15, :mode);
```

### 15.3.2 Case-specific regressors

```{julia}
#=
* Table of income by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(count income) statistic(mean income) statistic(sd income)
=#
# Income by fishing mode
DataFrames.combine(DataFrames.groupby(df15, :mode),
    :income => length      => :count,
    :income => Statistics.mean => :mean,
    :income => Statistics.std  => :sd)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Table of income by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(count income) statistic(mean income) statistic(sd income)
=#

# Stata-formatted version of the previous DataFrames.combine chunk:
# `stata_table` (chunk 1) reproduces Stata 17+ `table <byvar>,
# statistic(<stat> <var>) ...` output — one column per `(stat, var)`
# pair with auto headers `N(income)`, `Mean(income)`, `SD(income)`, a
# section row for the by-variable name, and a trailing Total row.
stata_table(df15, :mode,
    [(:count, :income), (:mean, :income), (:sd, :income)]);
```

### 15.3.3 Alternative-specific regressors

```{julia}
#=
* Table of fishing price by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(mean pbeach) statistic(mean ppier) statistic(mean pprivate) statistic(mean pcharter) nformat(%6.0f)
=#
# Mean fishing price by mode (mean of pbeach, ppier, pprivate, pcharter)
DataFrames.combine(DataFrames.groupby(df15, :mode),
    :pbeach   => Statistics.mean => :mean_pbeach,
    :ppier    => Statistics.mean => :mean_ppier,
    :pprivate => Statistics.mean => :mean_pprivate,
    :pcharter => Statistics.mean => :mean_pcharter)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Table of fishing catch rate by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(mean qbeach) statistic(mean qpier) statistic(mean qprivate) statistic(mean qcharter) nformat(%6.2f)
=#
# Mean catch rate by mode
DataFrames.combine(DataFrames.groupby(df15, :mode),
    :qbeach   => Statistics.mean => :mean_qbeach,
    :qpier    => Statistics.mean => :mean_qpier,
    :qprivate => Statistics.mean => :mean_qprivate,
    :qcharter => Statistics.mean => :mean_qcharter)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Table of fishing price by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(mean pbeach) statistic(mean ppier) statistic(mean pprivate) statistic(mean pcharter) nformat(%6.0f)
=#

# Same `stata_table` (chunk 1) helper, now with the Stata `nformat()`
# option threaded through: pass the raw Stata format string (`"%6.0f"`)
# and the helper feeds it to `Printf.format` to render every non-count
# cell as a fixed-point integer.
stata_table(df15, :mode,
    [(:mean, :pbeach), (:mean, :ppier),
     (:mean, :pprivate), (:mean, :pcharter)];
    nformat = "%6.0f");
```

```{julia}
#=
* Table of fishing catch rate by fishing mode
table mode, statistic(mean qbeach) statistic(mean qpier) statistic(mean qprivate) statistic(mean qcharter) nformat(%6.2f)
=#

# Same shape as the previous price-by-mode table, with the four catch-
# rate variables and `%6.2f` (fixed-point, 2 decimal places) instead.
stata_table(df15, :mode,
    [(:mean, :qbeach), (:mean, :qpier),
     (:mean, :qprivate), (:mean, :qcharter)];
    nformat = "%6.2f");
```

## 15.4 Multinomial logit model

### 15.4.1 Multinomial logit in Julia

There is no canonical Julia package for multinomial logit; we implement it
manually with `Optim.jl`.

### 15.4.2 Application

```{julia}
#=
* Multinomial logit with base outcome alternative 1
mlogit mode income, baseoutcome(1) nolog
=#

# `stata_mlogit` (chunk 1) fits a multinomial logistic regression by
# ML (LBFGS with autodiff gradient; OIM via FD Hessian) and prints
# Stata's `mlogit` output block — Number of obs / LR chi2(p) / Log
# likelihood / Pseudo R2, then one coefficient sub-block per outcome
# (base outcome flagged with `(base outcome)`). The returned
# NamedTuple binds `B` (K × J−1 coefficient matrix in `[_cons; regs...]`
# row order), `X`, `J`, and the cleaned `dfc` so the downstream
# §15.4.3–§15.4.5 chunks can read `β_mnl`, `X15`, `J`, `df15c` directly.
mlogit_res = stata_mlogit(df15, :mode, [:income]; baseoutcome = 1)
β_mnl  = mlogit_res.B
X15    = mlogit_res.X
J      = mlogit_res.J
df15c  = mlogit_res.dfc;
```

```{julia}
#=
* Wald test of the joint significance of income
test income
=#

# `stata_test_mlogit` (chunk 1) is Stata's post-`mlogit` `test <var>`:
# joint Wald test that the income coefficient equals zero in *every*
# outcome equation. Stata enumerates one constraint per outcome (the
# base-category row is flagged with `o.` for "omitted"), drops the
# base constraint, and reports `chi2(J-1)` / `Prob > chi2` on the
# remaining (J-1) active restrictions.
stata_test_mlogit(mlogit_res, :income);
```

### 15.4.3 Coefficient interpretation

```{julia}
#=
* Relative-risk option reports exp(b) rather than b
mlogit mode income, rr baseoutcome(1) nolog
=#
# Relative-risk ratios = exp(β)
println("\n— Relative-risk ratios (exp(β)) —")
for (j, alt) in enumerate(("pier", "private", "charter"))
    Printf.@printf("%-10s  exp(intercept) = %9.6f   exp(β_income) = %9.6f\n",
            alt, exp(β_mnl[1, j]), exp(β_mnl[2, j]))
end
```

```{julia}
#=
* Relative-risk option reports exp(b) rather than b
mlogit mode income, rr baseoutcome(1) nolog
=#

# Same `stata_mlogit` (chunk 1) call, with `rrr = true` to mirror Stata's
# `, rr` option: the coefficient column switches to "RRR" and reports
# exp(β); SE uses the delta method (exp(β)·SE(β)); z and P>|z| are
# unchanged (testing β = 0 ⇔ RRR = 1); CI endpoints are exp(β ± crit·SE(β)).
stata_mlogit(df15, :mode, [:income]; baseoutcome = 1, rrr = true);
```

### 15.4.4 Predicted probabilities

```{julia}
#=
// Following used below
estimates store MNL
=#
#=
// Following used below
estimates store MNL
=#

# `estimates store MNL` snapshots the current estimation results under
# the name "MNL" for later retrieval (e.g., `hausman MNL CL`). In Julia
# we just bind the full NamedTuple returned by `stata_mlogit` to a
# variable — `MNL.B`, `MNL.V`, `MNL.X`, `MNL.J`, etc. are all
# accessible by downstream sections.
MNL = mlogit_res;
```

```{julia}
#=
* Predict probabilities of choice of each mode and compare to actual freqs
predict pmlogit1 pmlogit2 pmlogit3 pmlogit4, pr
summarize pmlogit* dbeach dpier dprivate dcharter, separator(4)
=#
#=
* Predict probabilities of choice of each mode and compare to actual freqs
predict pmlogit1 pmlogit2 pmlogit3 pmlogit4, pr
summarize pmlogit* dbeach dpier dprivate dcharter, separator(4)
=#

# `StatEcon._c15_mnl_probs` (chunk 1) evaluates the fitted MNL probabilities
# P(y = j | x) for each outcome j — Stata's `predict pmlogit*, pr`.
P_mnl = StatEcon._c15_mnl_probs(β_mnl, X15, J)
df15c.pmlogit1 = P_mnl[:, 1]
df15c.pmlogit2 = P_mnl[:, 2]
df15c.pmlogit3 = P_mnl[:, 3]
df15c.pmlogit4 = P_mnl[:, 4]

# Indicator dummies for the actual choice (mirrors Stata's pre-built
# `dbeach`/`dpier`/`dprivate`/`dcharter` — easier than relying on the
# dataset's pre-computed columns and uses the same `_rawval` unwrap we
# need everywhere else for labeled outcomes).
df15c.dbeach    = Float64.(_rawval.(df15c.mode) .== 1)
df15c.dpier     = Float64.(_rawval.(df15c.mode) .== 2)
df15c.dprivate  = Float64.(_rawval.(df15c.mode) .== 3)
df15c.dcharter  = Float64.(_rawval.(df15c.mode) .== 4)

# Stata's `separator(4)` only controls the dashed line drawn every N
# vars in long varlists; our `stata_summarize` helper never emits that
# separator, so it's a no-op here.
stata_summarize(df15c,
    [:pmlogit1, :pmlogit2, :pmlogit3, :pmlogit4,
     :dbeach, :dpier, :dprivate, :dcharter]);
```

### 15.4.5 Marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
* Sample average predicted probability of the third outcome
margins, predict(outcome(3)) noatlegend
=#

# `stata_margins_mlogit` (chunk 1) computes Stata's `margins,
# predict(outcome(<k>)) noatlegend` after `mlogit`: the sample-average
# predicted probability P̄ = (1/N) Σᵢ P̂ᵢ(outcome) with delta-method SE
# from the closed-form per-obs gradient ∂Pᵢ(j)/∂β and `MNL.V`.
# `noatlegend` only suppresses the at-values legend block (which we
# don't emit anyway), so it's a no-op here.
stata_margins_mlogit(MNL; outcome = 3);

```

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effect at mean of income change for outcome 3
margins, dydx(*) predict(outcome(3)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# `stata_margins_dydx_mlogit` (chunk 1) computes Stata's
# `margins, dydx(*) predict(outcome(<k>)) atmean noatlegend`: the
# closed-form MNL partial derivative
#     ∂Pᵢ(target)/∂x_c = Pᵢ(target)·(β_{target,c} − Σₗ β_{l,c}·Pᵢ(l))
# evaluated at the sample means of `X` (`at = :mean`), with a delta-
# method SE built from a central-FD Jacobian w.r.t. vec(B) and `MNL.V`.
stata_margins_dydx_mlogit(MNL; outcome = 3, at = :mean);

```

```{julia}
#=
* Average marginal effect of income change for outcome 3
margins, dydx(*) predict(outcome(3)) noatlegend
=#

# Same `stata_margins_dydx_mlogit` (chunk 1) helper, with
# `at = :asobserved` to get the *average* marginal effect: evaluate
# the closed-form ∂Pᵢ(target)/∂x_c at every estimation-sample row and
# average. SE is delta-method on the gradient of that average.
# Stata's title switches from "Conditional marginal effects" (atmean)
# to "Average marginal effects" — the helper does the same.
stata_margins_dydx_mlogit(MNL; outcome = 3, at = :asobserved);

```

## 15.5 Conditional logit model

### 15.5.1 Wide-form to long-form conversion

```{julia}
#=
* Data are in wide form
list mode price pbeach ppier pprivate pcharter in 1, clean
=#

# `stata_list` (chunk 1) is Stata's `list <varlist> in <range>, clean`:
# clean format (no border/`|` separators), obs-number prefix, %9.0g
# numerics, and labeled values unwrapped to their string label. Stata's
# `in 1` picks just the first row.
stata_list(df15, [:mode, :price, :pbeach, :ppier, :pprivate, :pcharter];
           in_range = 1);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Convert data from wide form to long form
generate id = _n
reshape long d p q, i(id) j(fishmode beach pier private charter) string
save musr/mus15datalong.dta, replace
=#

# Stata's wide→long reshape: for each obs `id ∈ 1..N`, create one row
# per alternative (beach, pier, private, charter), with `d`/`p`/`q`
# pulled from `d<mode>`/`p<mode>`/`q<mode>` and `fishmode` the string
# alternative label. Per-obs invariants (`mode`, `income`) are
# replicated across the 4 long rows so `clogit_nll` can read them on
# any single row of a group.
df15.id = 1:DataFrames.nrow(df15)
df15long = DataFrames.DataFrame()
for m in ("beach", "pier", "private", "charter")
    DataFrames.append!(df15long, DataFrames.DataFrame(
        id       = df15.id,
        fishmode = fill(m, DataFrames.nrow(df15)),
        d        = df15[!, Symbol("d", m)],
        p        = df15[!, Symbol("p", m)],
        q        = df15[!, Symbol("q", m)],
        mode     = df15.mode,
        income   = df15.income,
    ))
end
DataFrames.sort!(df15long, [:id])

# Stata's `save … , replace` writes the long file to disk for later
# scripts; in Julia `df15long` lives in memory and downstream chunks
# read it directly. A CSV mirror is kept on disk for parity (the
# textbook's downstream Stata sessions reload from .dta — Julia
# sessions can `CSV.read("musr/mus15datalong.csv", DataFrame)` instead).
CSV.write("$(DATA)/musr/mus15datalong.csv", df15long);
```

```{julia}
#=
* List data for the first case after reshape
list in 1/4, clean noobs
=#

# Same `stata_list` (chunk 1) helper applied to *every* column of
# `df15long` for rows 1..4 — the four alternative rows of the first
# id. `noobs = true` suppresses the obs-number prefix to match
# Stata's `, clean noobs`.
stata_list(df15long, Symbol.(DataFrames.names(df15long));
           in_range = 1:4, noobs = true);
```

### 15.5.2 Conditional logit (manual)

### 15.5.3 The dogit model

### 15.5.4 Application

```{julia}
#=
* Conditional logit with alternative-specific and case-specific regressors
asclogit d p q, case(id) alternatives(fishmode) casevars(income) basealternative(beach) nolog
=#

# `stata_asclogit` (chunk 1) fits Stata's alternative-specific
# conditional logit on the long-form `df15long`: utility
#     U_ij = β_p·p_ij + β_q·q_ij + α_j + γ_j·income_i + ε_ij
# with `beach` pinning α_beach = γ_beach = 0. The returned NamedTuple
# binds `β_cl` (the concatenated parameter vector `[β_p; β_q;
# α_pier,α_private,α_charter; γ_pier,γ_private,γ_charter]`) so the
# downstream §15.5.7 / §15.5.8 chunks can call `StatEcon._c15_clogit_probs(β_cl, …)`
# directly.
asclogit_res = stata_asclogit(df15long;
    case_var      = :id,
    alt_var       = :fishmode,
    depvar        = :d,
    alt_specific  = [:p, :q],
    case_vars     = [:income],
    basealt       = "beach")
β_cl = asclogit_res.β_cl;
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following used below
estimates store CL
=#

# Stata `estimates store CL` snapshots the current `asclogit` fit for
# later retrieval (e.g., the `hausman MNL CL` IIA test in §15.5.5).
# In Julia we just bind the NamedTuple returned by `stata_asclogit` —
# `CL.β_cl`, `CL.V`, `CL.alts`, `CL.nonbase`, etc. all accessible
# downstream.
CL = asclogit_res;
```

### 15.5.5 Relationship to multinomial logit

### 15.5.6 Coefficient interpretation

### 15.5.7 Predicted probabilities

```{julia}
#=
* Predicted probabilities of choice of each mode and compare to actual freqs
*capture drop pasclogit
predict pasclogit, pr
table fishmode, statistic(mean d) statistic(mean pasclogit) statistic(sd pasclogit)
=#
#=
* Predicted probabilities of choice of each mode and compare to actual freqs
*capture drop pasclogit
predict pasclogit, pr
table fishmode, statistic(mean d) statistic(mean pasclogit) statistic(sd pasclogit)
=#

# `predict pasclogit, pr` after `asclogit` is the same per-(case,
# alternative) softmax that `StatEcon._c15_clogit_probs` (chunk 1) implements:
# stable softmax over each case's J utilities given `β_cl`. The Stata
# `table` then aggregates by `fishmode` — mean(d) recovers the
# empirical share per alternative, and mean(pasclogit) /
# sd(pasclogit) summarise the fitted probabilities. Both go through
# `stata_table` (chunk 1) for a Stata-formatted block.
df15long.pasclogit = StatEcon._c15_clogit_probs(β_cl, df15long)
stata_table(df15long, :fishmode,
    [(:mean, :d), (:mean, :pasclogit), (:sd, :pasclogit)]);
```

### 15.5.8 Marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
estat alternatives
=#

# `stata_estat_alternatives` (chunk 1) is Stata's post-`asclogit`
# diagnostic: per-alternative counts (cases-available, % cases, times-
# chosen, % chosen). Reads alternatives from `CL.alts` (order in which
# `stata_asclogit` first saw them in the data) and tallies against
# `df15long`.
stata_estat_alternatives(CL, df15long;
    depvar = :d, alt_var = :fishmode, case_var = :id);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effect at mean of change in price
estat mfx, varlist(p)
=#

# `stata_estat_mfx_asclogit` (chunk 1) is Stata's `estat mfx,
# varlist(<var>)` after `asclogit`. At the per-alternative means of
# all regressors, it reports the closed-form alt-specific marginal
# effect
#     ∂P̄_k / ∂x_j = P̄_k · (δ_kj − P̄_j) · β_var
# for every (target outcome k, varied alternative j) pair — Stata's
# "own" (k = j) and "cross" (k ≠ j) effects — with delta-method SE
# from a numerical Jacobian on `CL.V`.
stata_estat_mfx_asclogit(CL, df15long; var = :p);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Alternative-specific example: AME of beach price change computed manually
preserve
quietly summarize p
generate delta = r(sd)/1000
quietly replace p = p + delta if fishmode == "beach"
predict pnew, pr
generate dpdbeach = (pnew - pasclogit)/delta
tabulate fishmode, summarize(dpdbeach)
restore
=#

# Manual finite-difference AME of beach price change. Stata's
# `preserve`/`restore` is a temporary-edit pattern — we get the same
# effect by working on a `copy` of `df15long`. The step δ is the
# textbook's `sd(p)/1000`. The Stata `tabulate fishmode,
# summarize(dpdbeach)` produces mean / SD / freq of `dpdbeach` per
# alternative; we feed those three stats to `stata_table` (chunk 1).
df15long_p = copy(df15long)
δ = Statistics.std(df15long_p.p) / 1000
df15long_p.p[df15long_p.fishmode .== "beach"] .+= δ
df15long_p.pnew    = StatEcon._c15_clogit_probs(β_cl, df15long_p)
df15long_p.dpdbeach = (df15long_p.pnew .- df15long_p.pasclogit) ./ δ
stata_table(df15long_p, :fishmode,
    [(:mean, :dpdbeach), (:sd, :dpdbeach), (:count, :dpdbeach)]);
```

## 15.6 Nested logit model

### 15.6.1 Relaxing the IIA assumption

### 15.6.2 NL model

```{julia}
#=
* Define the Tree for Nested logit
*       with nesting structure 
*             /     \
*           /  \   /  \

* Convert string variable fishmode to integer values and attach labels
* encode fishmode, gen(intmode)
* label list intmode     // Check ordering of intmode
=#
#=
* Define the Tree for Nested logit
*       with nesting structure
*             /     \
*           /  \   /  \

* Convert string variable fishmode to integer values and attach labels
encode fishmode, gen(intmode)
label list intmode     // Check ordering of intmode
=#

# `stata_encode` (chunk 1) builds Stata's 1-based integer encoding of
# `df15long.fishmode` (sorted alphabetically — beach/charter/pier/
# private), writes it into `df15long.intmode`, and returns the
# `Int ⇒ String` label dictionary. `stata_label_list` then prints it
# in Stata's two-column `label list` layout.
intmode_lbl = stata_encode(df15long, :fishmode; gen = :intmode)
stata_label_list(intmode_lbl; name = "intmode");
```

### 15.6.3 Nested logit in Julia

### 15.6.4 Model estimates

```{julia}
#=
* Define the tree for nested logit
nlogitgen type = fishmode(shore: pier | beach, boat: private | charter)
=#
#=
* Define the tree for nested logit
nlogitgen type = fishmode(shore: pier | beach, boat: private | charter)
=#

# `stata_nlogitgen` (chunk 1) is Stata's `nlogitgen`: assigns each
# alternative to a nest, writes the integer-coded nest column into
# `df15long.type` (1 = shore, 2 = boat — left-to-right order in the
# call), and prints the standard "new variable … generated with 2
# groups" notice plus the `label list type` block.
type_lbl = stata_nlogitgen(df15long, :fishmode;
    gen   = :type,
    nests = ["shore" => ["pier", "beach"],
             "boat"  => ["private", "charter"]]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Check the tree
nlogittree fishmode type, choice(d)
=#
#=
* Check the tree
nlogittree fishmode type, choice(d)
=#

# `stata_nlogittree` (chunk 1) is Stata's `nlogittree`: lists the
# nesting tree with per-nest / per-alternative row counts and a `k`
# column (# times each alternative was chosen, i.e. `d == 1`). Passing
# the original `nests` vector preserves Stata's within-nest order
# (pier|beach for shore, private|charter for boat).
stata_nlogittree(df15long, :fishmode, :type;
    choice = :d,
    nests  = ["shore" => ["pier", "beach"],
              "boat"  => ["private", "charter"]]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Nested logit model estimate
nlogit d p q || type:, base(shore) || fishmode: income, case(id) notree nolog
=#
#=
* Nested logit model estimate
nlogit d p q || type:, base(shore) || fishmode: income, case(id) notree nolog
=#

# `stata_nlogit` (chunk 1) fits Stata's two-level nested logit by ML
# (LBFGS with autodiff gradient; dissimilarity τ_k re-parametrised
# through a logistic to keep it in (0, 1)). The chunk uses the same
# long-form `df15long` and nest spec we set up in §15.6.2. Stata
# picks the alphabetically-first alternative within the entire
# fishmode set as the global base — `beach` here — and we follow.
nlogit_res = stata_nlogit(df15long;
    case_var     = :id,
    alt_var      = :fishmode,
    nest_col     = :type,
    depvar       = :d,
    alt_specific = [:p, :q],
    case_vars    = [:income],
    basealt      = "beach",
    nests        = ["shore" => ["pier", "beach"],
                    "boat"  => ["private", "charter"]]);
```

### 15.6.5 Predicted probabilities

```{julia}
#=
// Following used below
estimates store NL
=#
#=
// Following used below
estimates store NL

* Predict level 1 and level 2 probabilities from NL model
predict plevel1 plevel2, pr
tabulate fishmode, summarize(plevel2)
=#

# Three Stata commands in one chunk:
#   • `estimates store NL` → bind `NL = nlogit_res` for downstream
#     `hausman MNL NL` (IIA test) and re-use.
#   • `predict plevel1 plevel2, pr` → `StatEcon._c15_nlogit_probs` (chunk 1) returns
#     the within-nest conditional (level-1) and the nest-level
#     marginal (level-2) probabilities, plus the joint.
#   • `tabulate fishmode, summarize(plevel2)` → `stata_table` with
#     mean / SD / N of `plevel2` by `fishmode`.
NL = nlogit_res
plv = StatEcon._c15_nlogit_probs(NL, df15long; case_var = :id, alt_var = :fishmode)
df15long.plevel1 = plv.plevel1   # P(nest)         — top level
df15long.plevel2 = plv.plevel2   # P(alternative)  — joint, bottom level

# `tabulate fishmode, summarize(plevel2)` — mean / SD / N of the
# unconditional alternative probability by fishmode. With this RUM-
# consistent nlogit, the per-alternative mean of plevel2 collapses
# to the empirical choice share (the same identity that holds for
# any saturated discrete-choice MLE).
stata_table(df15long, :fishmode,
    [(:mean, :plevel2), (:sd, :plevel2), (:count, :plevel2)]);

```

### 15.6.6 Marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
list d plevel1 plevel2 in 1/4, clean
estat alternatives
=#

# `stata_list` (chunk 1) for the first case's four alternative rows,
# then `stata_estat_alternatives` re-applied to the nlogit fit `NL`
# (the helper reads `res.alts` so it works for both `asclogit` and
# `nlogit` results).
stata_list(df15long, [:d, :plevel1, :plevel2]; in_range = 1:4)
stata_estat_alternatives(NL, df15long;
    depvar = :d, alt_var = :fishmode, case_var = :id);
```

```{julia}
#=
* AME of beach price change computed manually
preserve
quietly summarize p
generate delta = r(sd)/1000
quietly replace p = p + delta if fishmode == "beach"
predict pnew1 pnew2, pr
generate dpdbeach = (pnew2 - plevel2)/delta
tabulate fishmode, summarize(dpdbeach)
restore
=#

# Same manual-FD recipe used after `asclogit`, applied to the nested
# logit fit `NL`. `preserve`/`restore` is mimicked by working on a
# `copy(df15long)`. We bump only the beach rows' `p` by δ = SD(p)/1000,
# recompute the joint alternative probability via `StatEcon._c15_nlogit_probs`
# (Stata's `plevel2` = bottom-level joint), form (pnew2 − plevel2)/δ,
# and summarize by fishmode.
df15long_nl = copy(df15long)
δ_nl = Statistics.std(df15long_nl.p) / 1000
df15long_nl.p[df15long_nl.fishmode .== "beach"] .+= δ_nl
plv_new = StatEcon._c15_nlogit_probs(NL, df15long_nl; case_var = :id, alt_var = :fishmode)
df15long_nl.pnew2    = plv_new.plevel2
df15long_nl.dpdbeach = (df15long_nl.pnew2 .- df15long.plevel2) ./ δ_nl
stata_table(df15long_nl, :fishmode,
    [(:mean, :dpdbeach), (:sd, :dpdbeach), (:count, :dpdbeach)]);
```

### 15.6.7 Comparison of logit models

```{julia}
#=
* Summary statistics for the logit models
estimates table MNL CL NL, keep(p q) stats(N ll aic bic) equation(1) b(%7.3f) stfmt(%7.0f)
=#
#=
* Summary statistics for the logit models
estimates table MNL CL NL, keep(p q) stats(N ll aic bic) equation(1) b(%7.3f) stfmt(%7.0f)
=#

# `stata_estimates_table_compare` (chunk 1) compares the three stored
# fits side-by-side. The `keep` block looks up `p` and `q` in each
# model: MNL has no alt-specific p/q (case-specific-only mlogit), so
# its cells print as `.`; CL (`asclogit`) and NL (`nlogit`) report
# their β_alt coefficients. Statistics block (N / ll / aic / bic)
# uses `res.n_obs` (or `res.n` for MNL) and `res.nparam` for the IC
# formulas. Distinct from the older `estimates_table_stata` helper,
# which is for full regression-output tables with t-stats.
stata_estimates_table_compare([
    "MNL" => MNL,
    "CL"  => CL,
    "NL"  => NL
]; keep = [:p, :q]);

```

## 15.7 Multinomial probit model

### 15.7.1 MNP

### 15.7.2 The mprobit-style model

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* Multinomial Probit with independent errors and case-invariant regressors
use musr/mus15data.dta, clear
mprobit mode income, baseoutcome(1)
=#

# `stata_mprobit` (chunk 1) is the independent-errors multinomial
# probit: same case-specific design as `stata_mlogit` but with iid
# normal latent errors instead of Gumbel. The choice probability
# becomes a single 1-D integral
#     P(j*|i) = ∫ ∏_{k ≠ j*} Φ(z + V_ij* − V_ik) φ(z) dz
# evaluated by 20-point Gauss–Hermite quadrature. Estimation by ML
# (LBFGS + autodiff gradient), OIM via FD Hessian.
stata_mprobit(df15, :mode, [:income]; baseoutcome = 1);

```

### 15.7.3 Maximum simulated likelihood

### 15.7.4 The asmprobit-style model

### 15.7.5 Application

```{julia}
#=
* Multinomial probit wuth unstructured errors when charter is dropped
use musr/mus15datalong.dta, clear
drop if fishmode=="charter" | mode == 4
asmprobit d p q, case(id) alternatives(fishmode) casevars(income) correlation(unstructured) structural vce(robust)
=#

# Stata's full `asmprobit, correlation(unstructured) structural vce(robust)`
# is one of the most complex estimators in the package (BVN/MVN CDF
# integration + sandwich SEs); pure-Julia chunk-1 helpers we have
# implement the simpler **independent-errors** variant. Coefficients
# land in the right neighbourhood but won't exactly match Stata's
# unstructured fit — see `stata_asmprobit`'s docstring.
df15long_sub = filter(:fishmode => !=("charter"), df15long)
df15long_sub = filter(:mode => v -> _rawval(v) != 4, df15long_sub)

asmprobit_res = stata_asmprobit(df15long_sub;
    case_var     = :id,
    alt_var      = :fishmode,
    depvar       = :d,
    alt_specific = [:p, :q],
    case_vars    = [:income],
    basealt      = "beach");

```

```{julia}
#=
* Show correlations and covariance
estat correlation
estat covariance
=#

# `stata_estat_correlation` / `stata_estat_covariance` (chunk 1)
# after `stata_asmprobit`. Since our helper uses **independent
# errors**, the structural (J×J) matrices are the identity — no free
# correlation parameters. The differenced (J-1)×(J-1) form has 2 on
# the diagonal and 1 off-diagonal (correlation 0.5) because every
# differenced pair shares the base alternative's latent error.
stata_estat_correlation(asmprobit_res)
stata_estat_covariance(asmprobit_res);
```

### 15.7.6 Predicted probabilities and MEs

## 15.8 Random-parameters logit

### 15.8.1 Random-parameters logit

### 15.8.2 Mixed logit estimator

### 15.8.3 Data preparation

```{julia}
# Add case-invariant regressors interacted with alternative dummies
df15long.dpier    = Int.(df15long.fishmode .== "pier")
df15long.dprivate = Int.(df15long.fishmode .== "private")
df15long.dcharter = Int.(df15long.fishmode .== "charter")
df15long.ypier    = df15long.dpier    .* df15long.income
df15long.yprivate = df15long.dprivate .* df15long.income
df15long.ycharter = df15long.dcharter .* df15long.income
DataFrames.describe(df15long[:, [:dpier, :dprivate, :dcharter,
                                  :ypier, :yprivate, :ycharter]])
```

```{julia}
#=
* Data set up to include case-invariant regressors
use musr/mus15datalong.dta, clear
generate dbeach = fishmode=="pier"
generate dprivate = fishmode=="private"
generate dcharter = fishmode=="charter"
generate ybeach = dbeach*income
generate yprivate = dprivate*income
generate ycharter = dcharter*income
=#

# Translates the textbook's data-setup block literally, including the
# apparent typo on `dbeach` (Stata's code defines it as
# `fishmode == "pier"`, NOT `"beach"`; the others follow the matching
# alternative name). We preserve the snippet verbatim — flag the
# inconsistency rather than silently fix it.
df15long.dbeach   = Float64.(df15long.fishmode .== "pier")     # NB: matches textbook typo
df15long.dprivate = Float64.(df15long.fishmode .== "private")
df15long.dcharter = Float64.(df15long.fishmode .== "charter")
income_long       = Float64.(_rawval.(df15long.income))
df15long.ybeach   = df15long.dbeach   .* income_long
df15long.yprivate = df15long.dprivate .* income_long
df15long.ycharter = df15long.dcharter .* income_long;
```

### 15.8.4 Application

```{julia}
#=
*ssc install mixlogit
* Mixed logit or random parameters logit estimation
drop if fishmode=="charter" | mode == 4
capture noisily mixlogit d q dbeach dprivate ybeach yprivate, group(id) rand(p)
if _rc != 0 {
    display "Note: mixlogit convergence failed (flat region encountered)"
}
=#

# Same `stata_mixlogit` (chunk 1) call as §15.8.2 above, now run on the
# §15.8.3 data setup that builds `dbeach`/`dprivate`/`ybeach`/`yprivate`
# on `df15long` in-place (preserving the textbook typo where `dbeach`
# is `fishmode == "pier"`). We subset to drop the charter alt rows
# and the cases that chose charter, then fit with `p` as the random
# coefficient. See the §15.8.2 chunk for the local-optimum caveat —
# the LL will be close to Stata's reported value but the fixed-
# coefficient block may land at a different basin.
df15long_mix2 = filter(:fishmode => !=("charter"), df15long)
df15long_mix2 = filter(:mode => v -> _rawval(v) != 4, df15long_mix2)

stata_mixlogit(df15long_mix2;
    case_var    = :id,
    depvar      = :d,
    fixed_regs  = [:q, :dbeach, :dprivate, :ybeach, :yprivate],
    random_reg  = :p);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* Conditional logit using clogit as comparison
clogit d p q dbeach dprivate dcharter ybeach yprivate ycharter, group(id)
=#

# Stata's `clogit` runs on the charter-dropped subset that's already
# in memory from §15.8.4 (the `drop if fishmode=="charter" | mode==4`
# modifies the dataset in place). After that filter, `dcharter` and
# `ycharter` are identically zero so Stata flags them `(omitted)` —
# we just leave them out of `regs`. Because the §15.8.3 setup defines
# `dbeach = fishmode == "pier"` (textbook typo), the coefficient on
# `dbeach` is the *pier vs beach* contrast; with beach as the
# implicit base, both `dbeach` and `dprivate` get positive signs
# (matching the empirical shares pier > beach, private > beach).
df15long_clcmp = filter(:fishmode => !=("charter"), df15long)
df15long_clcmp = filter(:mode => v -> _rawval(v) != 4, df15long_clcmp)

stata_clogit(df15long_clcmp;
    case_var = :id,
    depvar   = :d,
    regs     = [:p, :q, :dbeach, :dprivate, :ybeach, :yprivate]);
```

## 15.9 Ordered outcome models

### 15.9.1 Data summary

```{julia}
#=
* Create multinomial ordered outcome variables takes values y = 1, 2, 3
use musr/mus18data.dta, clear
quietly keep if year==2
generate hlthpf = hlthp + hlthf
generate hlthe = (1 - hlthpf - hlthg)
quietly generate hlthstat = 1 if hlthpf == 1
quietly replace hlthstat = 2 if hlthg == 1
quietly replace hlthstat = 3 if hlthe == 1
label variable hlthstat "health status"
label define hsvalue 1 poor_or_fair 2 good 3 excellent
label values hlthstat hsvalue
tabulate hlthstat
=#
#=
* Create multinomial ordered outcome variables takes values y = 1, 2, 3
use musr/mus18data.dta, clear
quietly keep if year==2
generate hlthpf = hlthp + hlthf
generate hlthe = (1 - hlthpf - hlthg)
quietly generate hlthstat = 1 if hlthpf == 1
quietly replace hlthstat = 2 if hlthg == 1
quietly replace hlthstat = 3 if hlthe == 1
label variable hlthstat "health status"
label define hsvalue 1 poor_or_fair 2 good 3 excellent
label values hlthstat hsvalue
tabulate hlthstat
=#

# §15.9 switches dataset to `mus18data.dta` (medical-care panel) and
# keeps only year == 2 for the cross-section example. We construct
# the 3-level ordered outcome `hlthstat` (1 = poor_or_fair,
# 2 = good, 3 = excellent) from the binary health indicators, then
# print the value-label legend via `stata_label_list` and the
# frequency table via `stata_tabulate` (both chunk 1).
df18_y2 = let
    df = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus18data.dta"))
    DataFrames.filter(:year => v -> _rawval(v) == 2, df)
end
df18_y2.hlthpf = Int.(_rawval.(df18_y2.hlthp)) .+ Int.(_rawval.(df18_y2.hlthf))
df18_y2.hlthe  = 1 .- df18_y2.hlthpf .- Int.(_rawval.(df18_y2.hlthg))
df18_y2.hlthstat = [pf == 1 ? 1 : (g == 1 ? 2 : 3)
                    for (pf, g) in zip(df18_y2.hlthpf,
                                       Int.(_rawval.(df18_y2.hlthg)))]

hsvalue = Dict(1 => "poor_or_fair", 2 => "good", 3 => "excellent")
println("Variable label: hlthstat — \"health status\"")
stata_label_list(hsvalue; name = "hsvalue")
println()
stata_tabulate(df18_y2, :hlthstat);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Summarize dependent and explanatory variables
summarize hlthstat age linc ndisease
=#
#=
* Summarize dependent and explanatory variables
summarize hlthstat age linc ndisease
=#

# `linc` (= log(income)) is pre-computed in `mus18data.dta`, so we
# don't need to regenerate it — just feed the four variables directly
# to `stata_summarize` (chunk 1) for Stata's standard Obs / Mean /
# Std / Min / Max block.
stata_summarize(df18_y2, [:hlthstat, :age, :linc, :ndisease]);
```

### 15.9.2 Ordered outcomes

### 15.9.3 Ordered logit MLE

```{julia}
#=
* Ordered logit estimates
ologit hlthstat age linc ndisease, nolog
=#

# `stata_ologit` (chunk 1) fits an ordered logit by ML (LBFGS with
# autodiff gradient). Cut-points are parametrised as
# τ_1 = θ_{cut,1}, τ_j = τ_{j−1} + exp(θ_{cut,j}) so the optimiser
# never crosses cut-point ordering; SEs for τ are delta-method
# corrected. Returns β / τ / X / J / dfc and likelihood diagnostics
# for downstream `StatEcon._c15_ologit_probs` and marginal-effects chunks.
ologit_res = stata_ologit(df18_y2, :hlthstat, [:age, :linc, :ndisease])
β_ol   = ologit_res.β
τ_ol   = ologit_res.τ
X_ol   = ologit_res.X
J_o    = ologit_res.J
df18_ol = ologit_res.dfc;

```

### 15.9.4 Predicted probabilities

```{julia}
#=
* Calculate predicted probability that y=1, 2, or 3 for each person
predict p1ologit p2ologit p3ologit, pr
summarize hlthpf hlthg hlthe p1ologit p2ologit p3ologit, separator(0)
=#
#=
* Calculate predicted probability that y=1, 2, or 3 for each person
predict p1ologit p2ologit p3ologit, pr
summarize hlthpf hlthg hlthe p1ologit p2ologit p3ologit, separator(0)
=#

# `StatEcon._c15_ologit_probs` (chunk 1) is the ordered-logit `predict, pr`:
# P[:, j] = Λ(τ_j − Xβ) − Λ(τ_{j-1} − Xβ) with end-point handling.
# Stata's `separator(0)` only suppresses the every-5-rows dashed
# line in long summarize tables — `stata_summarize` (chunk 1) never
# emits that separator, so the option is a no-op.
P_ol = StatEcon._c15_ologit_probs(β_ol, τ_ol, X_ol, J_o)
df18_ol.p1ologit = P_ol[:, 1]
df18_ol.p2ologit = P_ol[:, 2]
df18_ol.p3ologit = P_ol[:, 3]

# Re-attach the binary health indicators to the cleaned subset
# (dropmissing kept only the model columns).
df18_ol.hlthpf = Int.(df18_ol.hlthstat .== 1)
df18_ol.hlthg  = Int.(df18_ol.hlthstat .== 2)
df18_ol.hlthe  = Int.(df18_ol.hlthstat .== 3)

stata_summarize(df18_ol,
    [:hlthpf, :hlthg, :hlthe, :p1ologit, :p2ologit, :p3ologit]);
```

### 15.9.5 Marginal effects

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effect at mean for 3rd outcome (health status excellent)
margins, dydx(*) predict(outcome(3)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# `stata_margins_dydx_ologit` (chunk 1) is Stata's `margins, dydx(*)
# predict(outcome(<j>)) atmean noatlegend` after `ologit`. Closed-
# form ∂P(y = j)/∂x_c = β_c · [λ(τ_{j−1} − x'β) − λ(τ_j − x'β)] at
# the column means of `res.X`, with delta-method SE from a central-FD
# Jacobian against `res.V` (which jointly covers β and τ).
stata_margins_dydx_ologit(ologit_res; outcome = 3, at = :mean);
```

### 15.9.6 Other ordered models

## 15.10 Multivariate outcomes

### 15.10.1 Bivariate probit

```{julia}
#=
* Two binary dependent variables: hlthe and dmdu
tabulate hlthe dmdu
correlate hlthe dmdu
=#
#=
* Two binary dependent variables: hlthe and dmdu
tabulate hlthe dmdu
correlate hlthe dmdu
=#

# `stata_tabulate_two_way` (chunk 1) — Stata's two-way `tabulate` with
# row/column/grand totals. Then `stata_correlate` (chunk 1) prints
# the standard Pearson correlation matrix. Both run on `df18_y2`
# which already carries the binary `hlthe` indicator built in §15.9.1
# and the original `dmdu` column from mus18.
stata_tabulate_two_way(df18_y2, :hlthe, :dmdu)
stata_correlate(df18_y2, [:hlthe, :dmdu]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Bivariate probit estimates
biprobit hlthe dmdu age linc ndisease, nolog
=#
#=
* Bivariate probit estimates
biprobit hlthe dmdu age linc ndisease, nolog
=#

# `stata_biprobit` (chunk 1) fits bivariate probit by ML using the
# equi-correlation decomposition: ε_1, ε_2 share a common factor Z
# weighted by √|ρ|, with the rest carried by independent η_j. That
# reduces the joint probability to a 1-D integral over Z which
# `integration_pts`-point Gauss–Hermite (default 24) handles cheaply.
# ρ is parametrised as `tanh(θ_ρ)` so the optimiser walks an
# unbounded line; SEs are delta-method-transformed back to (−1, 1).
# Both equations share the regressor list `[age, linc, ndisease]`.
biprobit_res = stata_biprobit(df18_y2, [:hlthe, :dmdu],
                              [:age, :linc, :ndisease]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Predicted probabilities
predict biprob1, pmarg1
predict biprob2, pmarg2
predict biprob11, p11
predict biprob10, p10
predict biprob01, p01
predict biprob00, p00
summarize hlthe dmdu biprob1 biprob2 biprob11 biprob10 biprob01 biprob00
=#
#=
* Predicted probabilities
predict biprob1, pmarg1
predict biprob2, pmarg2
predict biprob11, p11
predict biprob10, p10
predict biprob01, p01
predict biprob00, p00
summarize hlthe dmdu biprob1 biprob2 biprob11 biprob10 biprob01 biprob00
=#

# `StatEcon._c15_biprobit_probs` (chunk 1) returns the two marginals and the four
# joint cells of the 2×2 outcome table, computed via the same
# equi-correlation 1-D Gauss–Hermite scheme that `stata_biprobit`
# used at fit time. Attach the six columns to `df18_y2` and feed
# the eight variables (two indicators + six predictions) to
# `stata_summarize`. As a saturated MLE sanity check:
# mean(biprob1) ≈ mean(hlthe), mean(biprob2) ≈ mean(dmdu).
plv = StatEcon._c15_biprobit_probs(biprobit_res, df18_y2)
df18_y2.biprob1  = plv.pmarg1
df18_y2.biprob2  = plv.pmarg2
df18_y2.biprob11 = plv.p11
df18_y2.biprob10 = plv.p10
df18_y2.biprob01 = plv.p01
df18_y2.biprob00 = plv.p00
stata_summarize(df18_y2,
    [:hlthe, :dmdu, :biprob1, :biprob2,
     :biprob11, :biprob10, :biprob01, :biprob00]);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* Bivariate probit with different sets of regressors
biprobit (hlthe = age linc) (dmdu = age linc ndisease), nolog
=#
#=
// Following not included in book
* Bivariate probit with different sets of regressors
biprobit (hlthe = age linc) (dmdu = age linc ndisease), nolog
=#

# Same `stata_biprobit` (chunk 1) helper, now with a `regs2` keyword
# for the dmdu equation. `regs` carries the hlthe equation's
# regressors (age, linc); `regs2` adds `ndisease` for the dmdu
# equation. The negll, Hessian, and printout all index by per-
# equation `k1`/`k2` so the two regressor lists need not have the
# same length.
stata_biprobit(df18_y2, [:hlthe, :dmdu], [:age, :linc];
    regs2 = [:age, :linc, :ndisease]);
```

### 15.10.2 Nonlinear SUR

```{julia}
#=
* Nonlinear seemingly unrelated regressions estimator
nlsur (hlthe = normal({a1}*age+{a2}*linc+{a3}*ndisease+{a4})) ///
  (dmdu = normal({b1}*age+{b2}*linc+{b3}*ndisease+{b4})), vce(robust) nolog
=#
#=
* Nonlinear seemingly unrelated regressions estimator
nlsur (hlthe = normal({a1}*age+{a2}*linc+{a3}*ndisease+{a4})) ///
  (dmdu = normal({b1}*age+{b2}*linc+{b3}*ndisease+{b4})), vce(robust) nolog
=#

# `stata_nlsur` (chunk 1) — two-equation NLSUR where each
# conditional mean is `Φ(α'·[regs; 1])`. Two-step FGLS: unweighted NLS
# for an initial Σ̂, then re-minimise with Σ̂⁻¹ weighting. `vce =
# :robust` builds a sandwich SE from the per-obs score outer product.
stata_nlsur(df18_y2, [:hlthe, :dmdu],
    [:age, :linc, :ndisease]);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* Separate probits
probit hlthe age linc ndisease
probit dmdu age linc ndisease
=#
#=
// Following not included in book
* Separate probits
probit hlthe age linc ndisease
probit dmdu age linc ndisease
=#

# Two univariate probits as a benchmark for the joint biprobit and
# NLSUR fits above. `stata_probit` (chunk 1) is the Stata-style probit
# helper; we just run it once per outcome on the same `df18_y2`.
stata_probit(df18_y2, StatsModels.@formula(hlthe ~ age + linc + ndisease))
stata_probit(df18_y2, StatsModels.@formula(dmdu  ~ age + linc + ndisease));
```

## 15.11 Stata resources
